clean_title: armv8.1 lse regression for armv8.0 devices
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug pytorch 2.10.0 crashes on armv8.0 processors (cortex-a53/a72/a73) due to lse (large system extensions) instructions in libc10.so. this is a regression - issue reported this problem and claimed it was fixed in 2.4.1, but the issue has returned in 2.10.0. environment • hardware: arm cortex-a53 (armv8.0 architecture) • cpu features: fp asimd evtstrm aes pmull sha1 sha2 crc32 cpuid (notably missing lse) • os: debian trixie (testing), linux 6.12.63 • architecture: aarch64 (arm64) • pytorch version: 2.10.0 • installation method: pip (pip install torch) • python version: 3.13 error description any attempt to import or use pytorch results in sigill (illegal instruction) due to lse atomic instructions that are not supported on armv8.0 processors. error message illegal instruction (core dumped) gdb backtrace program received signal sigill, illegal instruction. 0x0000ffffbe27f3c8 in mi options init () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so (gdb) bt 0x0000ffffbe27f3c8 in mi options init () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so 0x0000ffffbe2755d0 in mi process init () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so 0x0000ffffbe27560c in mi process load () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so 0x0000ffffbf7df180 in call init (env= , argv= , argc= , l= ) at ./elf/dl-init.c:74 call init (l= , argc=1, argv=0xfffffffff548, env=0xfffffffff558) at ./elf/dl-init.c:26 0x0000ffffbf7df2b8 in dl init (main map=0xffffbf7ff040, argc=1, argv=0xfffffffff548, env=0xfffffffff558) at ./elf/dl-init.c:121 0x0000ffffbf7fa660 in dl start user () from /lib/ld-linux-aarch64.so.1 disassembly at crash point (gdb) x/5i $pc = 0xffffbe27f3c8 < mi options init+1064 : ldaddal x1, x2, [x0] ; ← lse instruction (sigill) 0xffffbe27f3cc < mi options init+1068 : cmp x2, x0 0xffffbe27f3d0 < mi options init+1072 : b.ne 0xffffbe27f014 < mi options init+100 0xffffbe27f3d4 < mi options init+1076 : adrp x0, 0xffffbe465000 0xffffbe27f3d8 < mi options init+1080 : add x0, x0, x9c8 the ldaddal instruction is part of the armv8.1 lse extension and is not supported on armv8.0 processors like cortex-a53. analysis lse instruction count in libc10.so $ objdump -d /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so | \ grep -e 'ldadd|ldclr|ldeor|ldset|swp|cas' | wc -l 22 the library contains 22 lse instructions, making it completely incompatible with armv8.0 processors. root cause: mimalloc dependency the crash occurs in mi options init() which is part of mimalloc (microsoft's memory allocator). pytorch's c10 library links against mimalloc-static: from c10/cmakelists.txt: if(use mimalloc) target link libraries(c10 private mimalloc-static) endif() it appears that the mimalloc build included in the pytorch wheel is compiled with lse instructions enabled, likely via -march=armv8.1-a or -march=armv8-a+lse flags. steps to reproduce 1. use any armv8.0 processor (cortex-a53, a57, a72, a73 - common in raspberry pi 3/4, many sbcs, older servers) 2. verify cpu lacks lse: grep -o 'atomics\|lse' /proc/cpuinfo (should return nothing) 3. install pytorch 2.10.0: pip install torch 4. try to import: python3 -c "import torch" 5. observe sigill crash regression information this is a regression from the fix mentioned in issue : • original issue: reported lse incompatibility on armv8.0 • claimed fix: said to be resolved in pytorch 2.4.1 • regression: the problem has returned in pytorch 2.10.0 the lse instructions should not be present in builds targeting general arm64 compatibility. the standard -march=armv8-a (without +lse) should be used for maximum compatibility. versions collecting environment information... pytorch version: n/a is debug build: n/a cuda used to build pytorch: n/a rocm used to build pytorch: n/a os: debian gnu/linux 13 (trixie) (aarch64) gcc version: (debian 14.2.0-19) 14.2.0 clang version: could not collect cmake version: version 3.31.6 libc version: glibc-2.41 python version: 3.13.5 (main, jun 25 2025, 18:55:22) [gcc 14.2.0] (64-bit runtime) python platform: linux-6.12.63+deb13-arm64-aarch64-with-glibc2.41 is cuda available: n/a cuda runtime version: could not collect cuda module loading set to: n/a gpu models and configuration: could not collect nvidia driver version: could not collect cudnn version: could not collect is xpu available: n/a hip runtime version: n/a miopen runtime version: n/a is xnnpack available: n/a caching allocator config: n/a cpu: architecture: aarch64 cpu op-mode(s): 32-bit, 64-bit byte order: little endian cpu(s): 4 on-line cpu(s) list: 0-3 vendor id: arm bios vendor id: arm limited model name: cortex-a53 bios model name: cpu @ 0.0ghz bios cpu family: 2 model: 4 thread(s) per core: 1 core(s) per cluster: 4 socket(s): 1 cluster(s): 1 stepping: r0p4 cpu(s) scaling mhz: 85% cpu max mhz: 1416.0000 cpu min mhz: 100.0000 bogomips: 48.00 flags: fp asimd evtstrm aes pmull sha1 sha2 crc32 cpuid numa node(s): 1 numa node0 cpu(s): 0-3 vulnerability gather data clean_title: allow github account preferences per profile for extensions (e.g. github copilot)
clean_body: type: feature request vs code version: 1.110.1 (commit [hash] , x64) os: ubuntu 24.04.2 lts, kernel 6.17.0-14-generic relevant extensions installed: github.copilot (built-in via vs code) github.copilot-chat vscode.github-authentication (built-in) feature request: currently, the preferred github account assigned to an extension is stored globally and shared across all profiles. switching profiles does not change the active account used by extensions like github copilot. expected behavior: each vs code profile should store its own preferred github account per extension, so switching profiles automatically uses the account associated with that profile — without manual intervention via "manage extension account preferences". example use case: profile default → github account personal-user → personal copilot license profile work → github account work-user → corporate copilot license reproducible steps: sign in to vs code with two different github accounts (e.g. user-a and user-b), both having a copilot license. create two profiles: default and work. in profile default, open the account menu (bottom-left) → "manage extension account preferences" → assign user-a to github copilot and github copilot chat. switch to profile work → open the account menu → "manage extension account preferences". assign user-b to github copilot and github copilot chat. switch back to profile default. what you expect to see: copilot uses user-a automatically in profile default. what you actually see: copilot still uses user-b (the last account set), regardless of the active profile. the account preference is not persisted per profile. root cause (investigated): the key github.copilot-github is stored in the global /.config/code/user/globalstorage/state.vscdb, not in the per-profile database at /.config/code/user/profiles/ /globalstorage/state.vscdb. vs code does not read or write this preference from the profile-scoped database when switching profiles. current workaround: the user must manually reassign the preferred account every time they switch profiles, which defeats the purpose of profiles for multi-identity workflows. why this matters: profiles are designed to provide isolated, context-specific environments. authentication preferences for extensions are a natural part of that context. without per-profile account binding, users with multiple github identities (e.g. personal + enterprise copilot licenses) cannot benefit from profiles for account isolation. dev tools console errors: none — this is a missing feature, not an error condition. vs code version: code 1.110.1 ( [hash] , 2026-03-06t23:03:27.520z) os version: linux x64 6.17.0-14-generic snap modes:

clean_title: tensorflow 2.20.0 causes mutex crash in huggingface tokenizers on macos arm without being imported
clean_body: issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? no source binary tensorflow version 2.20.0 custom code yes os platform and distribution macos (apple silicon / arm64) - mac studio tahoe 26.1 mobile device no response python version 3.13.11 bazel version no response gcc/compiler version clang 17.0.0 (clang-1700.4.4.1) cuda/cudnn version no response gpu model and memory apple silicon (mps backend) m2 ultra current behavior? simply having tensorflow 2.20.0 installed in a python environment causes a fatal mutex crash when using huggingface tokenizers, even though tensorflow is never imported in the code. when running code that loads a diffusers fluxpipeline (which uses huggingface tokenizers internally), the process crashes with: libc++abi: terminating due to uncaught exception of type std:: 1::system error: mutex lock failed: invalid argument this happens at the tokenizer loading stage, specifically when t5tokenizerfast is being initialized. expected behavior: tensorflow being installed but not imported should have no effect on unrelated libraries. the huggingface tokenizers library should load normally. key findings from debugging: 1. with tensorflow installed → crash occurs (tensorflow is never imported) 2. uninstall only tensorflow → works immediately 3. all tensorflow dependencies (keras, tensorboard, grpcio, h5py, etc.) can remain installed without issue 4. the issue is specifically the tensorflow package itself, not its dependencies 5. downgrading protobuf does not fix the issue 6. setting tokenizers parallelism=false does not fix the issue this suggests tensorflow 2.20.0 may be registering something at the native/shared library level that interferes with the rust-based tokenizers library's threading primitives on macos arm. standalone code to reproduce the issue [inline code] fluxpipeline [inline code] tensorflow [inline code] tensorflow [inline code] pip install torch torchvision diffusers transformers accelerate safetensors sentencepiece protobuf tensorflow 2. run the following python script: import torch from diffusers import fluxpipeline crash occurs during this call pipe = fluxpipeline.from pretrained( "black-forest-labs/flux.1-dev", torch dtype=torch.float16 ) pipe.to("mps") log output /users/username/venv-metal/lib/python3.13/site-packages/torch/amp/autocast mode.py:270: userwarning: user provided device type of 'cuda', but cuda is not available. disabling warnings.warn( loading pipeline components...: 0%| | 0/7 [00:00

all_comments_text: sorry for the awful formatting. i was trying to make it easier to read and ended up with the opposite being the case. my bad. if tf is installed, fluxpipeline crashes. if tf is not then it does not. that's all. all other dependencies can remain in the environment. |SEP| hi , thanks for reporting this issue! there are a few ongoing mutex/abi-related problems on macos/apple silicon with tensorflow, and your crash appears to fall under the same category. for further updates and fixes, please follow the existing upstream issue here: thank you!

clean_title: extension auto-update fails: downloaded .vsix is gzip-compressed but not decompressed
clean_body: summary vs code fails to install/update extensions from the marketplace with the error [inline code] the root cause is that the marketplace cdn returns [inline code] files with [inline code] , but vs code's download handler does not decompress the response body, resulting in a gzip stream being written to disk instead of the expected zip archive. vs code version [code block] steps to reproduce 1. configure a socks5 or http proxy in [inline code] : [code block] (also reproducible with [inline code] ) 2. let vs code auto-update extensions (or manually install via [inline code] ). 3. observe repeated errors in the log (help → toggle developer tools → console, or window log): [code block] 4. the same extensions can be downloaded correctly using [inline code] : [code block] root cause analysis the marketplace api endpoint returns responses with the following headers: [code block] the [inline code] header indicates transport-level compression. per http/1.1 (rfc 7231 §3.1.2.2), the client must decode the content-encoding before processing the payload. vs code's extension download path (via electron/node.js) does not handle this decompression. the raw gzip-compressed bytes are written directly as the [inline code] file. since [inline code] is a zip archive, the [inline code] library then fails to parse it. verification that this is proxy-independent: [code block] affected extensions (examples) all marketplace extensions are affected. tested with: - [inline code] - [inline code] - [inline code] - [inline code] relevant log output [code block] (error repeats in a loop as vs code retries the failed downloads.) expected behavior vs code should transparently decompress [inline code] responses before writing the [inline code] file to disk, or send an [inline code] header that prevents the server from applying content-encoding to binary payloads. workaround download extensions manually using [inline code] and install via cli: [code block]

clean_title: [heads-up] pytorch 2.9.x conv3d regression may significantly slow qwen3-vl vision forward
clean_body: this is not a qwen3-vl implementation bug, but an upstream pytorch behavior that may affect qwen3-vl users. we observed that upgrading pytorch from 2.8.x to 2.9.x can cause a 10–20× slowdown in vision-conditioned forward / generation for qwen3-vl, while text-only / llm-only inference remains unaffected. root cause (analysis) qwen3-vl vision patch embedding uses a conv3d layer (model.visual.patch embed.proj). on pytorch 2.9.1 (cuda 12.8), this conv3d may fall back to aten::slow conv dilated3d for certain shapes / gpus, instead of using a fast cudnn kernel. profiling shows this single conv3d dominates the entire vision forward. this behavior has been reported upstream in pytorch and appears to be resolved in pytorch 2.10. practical suggestions - if you see severe vision-side slowdowns, consider staying on pytorch 2.8.x - or upgrade to pytorch 2.10+ - be cautious when benchmarking qwen3-vl vision performance on pytorch 2.9.x posting this as a heads-up so users are aware of the upstream interaction. cc

clean_title: a note on numerical preconditions in softmax gradient translation-invariance testing
clean_body: issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? yes source source tensorflow version tf2.3.0 custom code yes os platform and distribution linux ubuntu 22.04 mobile device no response python version 3.7.12 bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version cudnn 8.6 gpu model and memory tesla v100 current behavior? hello tensorflow team, we’d like to report and clarify an observation from metamorphic testing of operator gradients, focusing on how large constant shifts interact with the numerical preconditions of certain metamorphic relations (mrs). we tested three operators under the same “constant shift” stress condition: avgpool2d (ap) maxpool2d (mp) softmax we observed that avgpool2d consistently satisfies the expected mr, while maxpool2d and softmax can exhibit reproducible deviations under large shifts. we have already reported the maxpool2d case separately; this email summarizes the broader finding and asks for guidance on how best to interpret/document it, especially for softmax. background (the mrs we tested) 1) constant shift transformation ( x' = x + c ) where (c) is constant along the mr-relevant axis (e.g., per-window constant for pooling, per-softmax slice constant for softmax), and may vary across other dimensions via broadcasting. 2) expected behavior avgpool2d: forward shifts by a constant; backward should be invariant under the same upstream gradient: ( gx' ≈ gx ) maxpool2d (with the precondition that argmax routing is preserved): ( gx' ≈ gx ) softmax (theoretically shift-invariant along the normalization axis): ( \mathrm{softmax}(x + c) = \mathrm{softmax}(x) ) and, under the same upstream gradient (g), ( gx' ≈ gx ) what we observed avgpool2d under large constant shifts (including magnitudes on the order of 1e4–1e5 in float32), avgpool2d did not show violations in our metamorphic checks (forward/gradient consistency remained within tolerance). maxpool2d under settings such as padding=same and stride=1, we observe reproducible mr violations with large violation metrics (vr) on both cpu and gpu when (|c|) is sufficiently large (e.g., hundreds or larger). this maxpool2d finding has already been reported. [url] softmax under large constant shifts (e.g., 1e4–1e5 in float32) applied as a constant along the softmax axis (via broadcasting), we observe reproducible numerical deviations in: forward output consistency and/or backward gradient consistency (gx) the effect appears implementation-path sensitive (e.g., cpu vs gpu and/or layout paths), though we can provide exact version/platform matrices and minimal repro scripts. our analysis / hypothesis we believe the maxpool2d behavior is expected under finite precision and ties: for large (|c|), float32 ulp becomes coarse relative to small differences. previously strictly ordered values can become equal or nearly equal after the shift (implicit ties). this can change argmax routing, hence backward routing changes and the mr precondition (“order preserved”) no longer holds. for softmax, we suspect a related but distinct numerical-stability / implementation-path-consistency issue: softmax is mathematically shift-invariant along the normalization axis, but under extreme magnitudes in float32, different kernel paths / reduction orders / stabilization behaviors can potentially lead to observable deviations. the fact that avgpool2d passes under the same stress condition suggests the phenomenon is not a general issue in the test harness, but operator-specific numerical behavior. questions we’d like to confirm the following points: for maxpool2d gradients, is it reasonable to treat violations under large constant shifts as not applicable (n/a) when the “argmax routing preserved” precondition is broken by finite-precision tie effects? for softmax, is it expected that extremely large constant shifts (float32) may lead to measurable forward/gradient deviations across device/layout implementations, or would you consider this worthy of investigation as an implementation-path consistency / numerical stability issue? would it be helpful to add a brief documentation note stating that large constant shifts can affect: maxpool gradient routing (due to finite-precision tie effects), and potentially softmax numerical consistency in extreme regimes, even though the underlying mathematical properties hold in real arithmetic? reproduction we have minimal deterministic scripts and concrete configurations (tf version, cpu/gpu, layout) for: maxpool2d (already reported), and softmax (new observation) we can provide them in this thread immediately if useful. thank you for your time and for the continued work on tensorflow. best regards, xiaohuaovo standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]

all_comments_text: hi -tech, i don't think the tf kernel is the problem here. the discrepancy in my opinion arises because the shifted f32 input cannot fully preserve the orignial input, leading to precision loss even before the inputs reach the tf kernel. eg: [code block] output: [code block] if i change the dtype to float64 in your reproducer then the test passes. |SEP| hi , ﻿ thanks again for the clear explanation and the reproduction script. ﻿ i completely agree with your assessment. i overlooked the fact that the large shift combined with [inline code] precision leads to significant information loss at the input level before the kernel execution, which naturally propagates to the gradient. ﻿ this is indeed a limitation of floating-point arithmetic rather than a logical bug in the operator implementation. i will refine my test case generation to respect the valid numerical domain of [inline code] .

clean_title: browser zoom: display zoom percent
clean_body: we should indicate the zoom percent as a temporary overlay or some other option. reference edge: chrome: vs code workbench: when zoomed in: when zoom at 100%: when zoomed out: current limitations we're limited with overlays because of the way integrated browser uses a floating webcontentsview so we might need to get creative to avoid having any overlays overlapping with the page itself. the way find in page feature works around the overlap issue is to push the whole page with a bar that appears: but that might not be ideal for displaying zoom since it will only be shown temporarily and will cause the page to shift.

clean_body: - copilot chat extension version: 0.37.9 - vs code version: 1.109.5 - os version: windows server 10.0.20348 (windows server 2022) - feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): agent mode - selected model (e.g. gpt 4.1, claude 3.7 sonnet): claude opus 4.6 - logs: (see attached - i hope!) steps to reproduce: (as self reported my friend claude herself) repro steps: ask copilot agent to edit a .prompt.md file that contains triple-backtick code fences as content expected: normal file edit actual: agent enters infinite text-generation loop (not a tool-calling loop) with alternating backtick markers and repeated phrases evidence: three separate occurrences in one session (you can reference the line numbers) root cause hypothesis: the read file tool wraps .prompt.md content in backtick fences with prompt as the language identifier, creating ambiguity when the file content itself contains backtick fences. the model cannot distinguish content from framing. attach (or excerpt) the relevant sections of exhibit c as evidence [exhibit c.md]( [url] see lines 281, 1309, 1319 | line | trigger | loop pattern -- | -- | -- | -- 1 | 281 | editing 04-end-of-day.prompt.md (replacement attempt) | "markers are part of the read file display formatting..." oscillating with [code block] markers 2 | 1309 | re-reading 04-end-of-day.prompt.md for the full rewrite | same root cause, second occurrence in the same file 3 | 1319 | editing 14-end-session.prompt.md | "fence structure:" oscillating with [code block] markers — the same class of bug but a different trigger file

clean_title: agents and task usage
clean_body: i had an agent that worked around sandbox limitations by using tasks. in the end the tasks.json looked like this [code block] the agent used [inline code] followed by [inline code] . however, the terminal was reused between runs and thus [inline code] returned the output of not just the last task run but also the task(s) before. thus, the agent was going through loops like "i see the test still fails, let me check if my changes were correctly applied. they were, the files look good. there must be stale files. let me rebuild and rerun. let's create a new task just to be sure." overall, it burnt a lot of tokens because of the reuse of the terminal for several tasks. here is an excerpt of the behavior: [url] here's the result of the [inline code] analysis: [url]

clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug summary i know [inline code] / [inline code] have been deprecated (and the warning in the logs makes that clear), so i’m mostly filing this as a “heads up” in case they’re still expected to degrade gracefully rather than segfault. the repro involves a tiny model with a registered buffer constant. export + aot compile succeed, but [inline code] fails during constant loading with: [code block] and then the process terminates with a segmentation fault. even for deprecated apis, this feels like it should probably error out cleanly instead of crashing. --- minimal reproduction [code block] --- actual output [code block] the stack trace shows the failure happening inside aotinductormodelbase constant loading, then a crash in the cuda path after [inline code] . --- expected behavior given that this api is deprecated, i wouldn’t expect correctness fixes or new features, but i would expect at least one of: a clean python exception, or a clear “unsupported” error, or even a graceful no-op fallback rather than a segfault. the crash path seems related to constants being treated as host pointers during aoti constant loading. this also repros even without backward/aotautograd complexity — just export → aot compile → aot load. the deprecation warning points to: [code block] which i’ll use going forward, but reporting the segfault just in case someone still depends on the old api for legacy workloads. versions [code block] cc -amd

clean_title: numerical divergence: conv2d produces nan on cuda but finite results on cpu with identical float32 input
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug i encountered a numerical stability issue in nn.conv2d. with binary-identical input and weights (input consistency check = 0.0), the cuda backend produces nan, while the cpu backend produces valid finite results. the input values are near the float32 limit ($3.17 \times 10^{38}$) but are still finite. this suggests an inconsistency or instability in the cuda convolution kernel implementation (potentially in cudnn or generated kernels) when handling edge-case accumulations. all_comments_text: seems related to [url] |SEP| i think this is covered in [url] |SEP| "i’d like to clarify that the core issue is the inconsistency of the error state across different execution modes. observed divergence: for the same fp32 input, the cpu produces a finite result ($3.15 \times 10^{38}$) while cuda produces nan. inconsistent behavior: as noted in pytorch documentation regarding extremal values (e.g., intermediate overflows in norm()), while results may vary, the existence of the error state itself should be consistent. switching execution settings (cpu vs. cuda) changes whether an error occurs at all, which violates backend parity. this functional discrepancy goes beyond 'slightly different results' and warrants a re-evaluation based on the requirement for consistent behavior between backends."

clean_title: [inconsistency] inductor backend produces numerically divergent results vs eager for aten.cumprod backward
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug describe the bug when compiling a model containing aten.cumprod backward with torch.compile, the inductor backend produces results that are numerically drastically different from the eager backend. the discrepancy affects 100% of elements and includes extremely large absolute and relative errors. this appears to be a systematic divergence rather than random floating-point noise, and is likely related to numerical instability and/or computation reordering in the inductor lowering of cumprod backward. minimal reproduction [code block] results [code block] versions [code block] cc -chen -nrv

clean_title: dtensor shard- replicate redistribution corrupts symbolic shapes under torch.compile
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug when a dtensor with shard(dim) placement on a dynamically-sized dimension is redistributed to replicate, the gathered dimension's symbolic shape becomes n ceil(s/n) instead of s. this corrupted expression propagates through all downstream operations (view, transpose, reshape, to local), causing shape mismatches in compilation pipelines. root cause: shard. to replicate tensor in placement types.py 1. torch.chunk uses ceiling division: local size = (s + n - 1) // n 2. all gather concatenates n chunks: gathered = n ((s + n - 1) // n) 3. maybe unpad tensor checks "s % n != 0" — evaluates false for even s, so no unpadding occurs 4. result has symbolic size n ceil(s/n) ≠ s (sympy cannot simplify) reproduction run: [inline code] with the following ode as clean_title: [inductor] mismatch between eager and compiled outputs when converting fp32 → bf16 → fp32
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug issue description when compiling a function that performs a round-trip type conversion (fp32 → bfloat16 → fp32) using [inline code] , the output differs from the eager mode execution. reproduction code [code block] the assertion fails with a mismatch between eager and compiled outputs. generated triton kernel i tried to disable the registered replace pattern [inline code] in [url] and get the following generated triton kernel: [code block] the generated kernel completely skips the fp32 → bf16 → fp32 conversion chain. it directly loads fp32 values and stores them back as fp32, which is inconsistent with eager mode. while the inductor's optimization might produce numerically better results, we have a use case that requires strict bitwise equivalence with eager mode. is there a way to disable this specific optimization or enforce that type casts are preserved in the generated code? cc -chen -nrv versions torch 2.9.1

all_comments_text: hi -mel, i have tested importing [inline code] in colab, its working fine in this [gist]( [url] i have also tried importing tensorflow 2.20 in my local env with python 3.11 and i am able to import it without any issues. the issue seems to be caused by a tensorflow, protobuf binary mismatch in the environment, can you try recreating the environment and downgrading the python version. let me know if you face any issues. |SEP| thanks ,i resolved the binary mismatch issue by uninstalling python from all installations, including anaconda, and then reinstalling anaconda along with tensorflow. initially, the kernel kept crashing and restarting due to tensorflow. then, again uninstalled tensorflow using pip and then reinstalled it using conda. after this, the package started working correctly.
solution_comments: thanks ,i resolved the binary mismatch issue by uninstalling python from all installations, including anaconda, and then reinstalling anaconda along with tensorflow. initially, the kernel kept crashing and restarting due to tensorflow. then, again uninstalled tensorflow using pip and then reinstalled it using conda. after this, the package started working correctly.

all_comments_text: hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising this issue. i was able to reproduce the same behavior on colab using tensorflow 2.19.0 and 2.20.0. the main cause is the argmax tensor you are passing. according to the documentation, argmax contains the indices of the maximum values chosen by the forward [inline code] op. this means the values must be valid flattened indices within the expected range for the input tensor. in your code, the argmax value is far outside the valid range, which leads to undefined behavior and results in the illegal memory access reported by compute sanitizer. when i replace argmax with a valid flattened index, the code works correctly. i am attaching a colab [gist]( [url] with the working version for your reference. hope this helps thank you!

clean_title: o(n^2) in torchscript onnx exporter initializer deduplication pass
clean_body: there is a o(n^2) loop in torchscript onnx exporter initializer deduplication pass, which dominates export time of large models, including most huggingface llms. (n: number of initializers) the quadraticity comes from all-to-all comparison between all initializers: [url] since 1) [inline code] is called once for each of n initializers, and 2) [inline code] is a vector of initializers, this loop is o(n^2). although [inline code] function often only compares tensor metadata (shape, dtype, etc) and not actually compare the values of the tensors if the metadata didn't match, this loop starts to dominates the export time of large models with many initializers. in my observation, 1k initializers seems to be the tipping point, which is the case for most llms. reproducible example [code block] output [code block] suggestion define [inline code] as [inline code] instead of [inline code] with a proper hash function. with this, the deduplication pass can be significantly optimized, possibly into o(n). these are the possible hash keys for hashing [inline code] : tensor metadata -- shape, dtype, strides, ... rationale: this will reduce the export time by a significant constant factor. however, the time complexity remains o(n^2) if the number of tensors with same shape and dtype increases with n (which is true in llms) first element of the tensor rationale: most tensors differ from one another from the very first element. with a practical assumption that $p(x 1 = y 1 \rightarrow x = y) \approx 1$ , this will optimize the deduplication pass into o(n) in most practical cases. cc -nrv

clean_title: token consumption loop
clean_body: type: bug i mistakenly assumed that the suggested /create-instructions command would generate a guidelines file for copilot. so i clarified that it should create the file based on the existing .cursorrules in my project. this was transformed into the following instruction: “follow instructions in create-instructions.prompt.md based on the file @.cursorrules.” this led to a prolonged drain of tokens and a stream of gibberish output, consuming 28% of my monthly allowance in a single request. it would drain even more if i didn't explicitly stop it. i acknowledge that this prompt didn't make any sense, but still, it's concerning that such loops might occur so easily. so i decided to share. i hope it helps to make the service better. extension version: 0.38.2 vs code version: code 1.110.1 ( [hash] , 2026-03-06t23:03:27.520z) os version: windows nt x64 10.0.26200 modes: system info |item|value| |cpus|amd ryzen 5 220 w/ radeon 740m graphics (12 x 3194)| |gpu status|2d canvas: enabled gpu0: vendor= 0x1002, device=0x1901 [amd radeon 740m graphics], driver vendor=amd, driver version=32.0.13062.3005 active gpu1: vendor= 0x1414, device=0x008c [microsoft basic render driver], driver version=10.0.26100.7309 machine model name: machine model version: direct rendering display compositor: disabled off ok gpu compositing: enabled multiple raster threads: enabled on opengl: enabled on rasterization: enabled raw draw: disabled off ok skia graphite: disabled off trees in viz: disabled off video decode: enabled video encode: enabled webgl: enabled webgl2: enabled webgpu: enabled webnn: disabled off| |load (avg)|undefined| |memory (system)|15.26gb (1.66gb free)| |process argv|--crash-reporter-id 613837d5-5653-4690-b5d4- [hash] | |screen reader|no| |vm|0%| a/b experiments [code block]

all_comments_text: [code block] [url] linker the linker now generates a gnu build id (the elf nt gnu build id note) on elf platforms and a uuid (the mach-o lc uuid load command) on macos by default. the build id or uuid is derived from the go build id. it can be disabled by the -b none linker flag, or overridden by the -b 0xnnnn linker flag with a user-specified hexadecimal value. [url] |SEP| are we using go? |SEP| no, i just hoped that nice documentation entry could help implementing the missing lc uuid support. |SEP| are there any plans to fix this issue? |SEP| i don't know what tensorflow lite is. this issue is for building tensorflow, not litert. |SEP| hi , my apologies. this has now been assigned to the right person for support. thanks. |SEP| i guess someone mixed up this tensorflow issue with [url] though the issue template states [inline code] for example.

all_comments_text: i've recently worked on constant folding for extract patches and would like to investigate this issue |SEP| hi -tree , apologies for the delay, and thank you for raising this issue. i ran your code on tensorflow 2.21.0-rc0 and the nightly version. while i encountered the same issues on 2.21.0-rc0, i observed that the problem has been resolved in the nightly build. therefore, this fix will be included in upcoming versions. i have attached a working colab [gist]( [url] for your reference. thank you! |SEP| technical note on root cause and best practices it is great to see that the issue is mitigated in the tf 2.21 nightly build and that the compiler now handles this state without crashing. however, from an architectural standpoint, we should distinguish between a "crash fix" and a "performant implementation." the performance aspect: using python primitives like random.randint to determine shapes often forces the compiler into frequent re-tracing. each time the python value changes, xla may see it as a new graph signature, leading to unnecessary compilation overhead. recommendation for stability and speed: even with the latest fixes, it remains best practice to use native tensorflow semantics (e.g., tf.shape(x)[0]) instead of python-side generators. logical stability: ensures the graph is portable across cpu, gpu, and tpu backends. optimal performance: by using tf.shape, the dimensions remain symbolic within the same compiled executable. this avoids the "re-compilation trap" and allows xla to optimize the hardware kernels once for all input sizes. while the nightly fix prevents the failure, using tf.shape ensures the system remains lean and statically sound.

clean_title: [torch.compile] massive numerical discrepancy ( 0.05 max abs error) in conv+bn+silu fusion with inductor fallback random=1
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug when using torch.compile with the inductor backend and enabling inductor fallback random=1, there is a significant numerical divergence compared to eager mode. in a standalone reproduction involving the first three blocks of a yolov8-like architecture (conv + bn + silu), the max absolute error reaches 0.05, and the max relative error exceeds 10^5. this suggests an unstable numerical approximation within the fused kernels. observed results: comparing mode a (eager execution) vs mode b (compiled with inductor fallback random=1): max absolute error: 5.4588e-02 max relative error: 1.0962e+05 rel l2 error (global): 3.8443e-04 eager output range: [-0.2785, 97.1013] compiled output range: [-0.2785, 97.0889] why this matters: while a 0.05 absolute error might seem small in isolation, this discrepancy is roughly 4 orders of magnitude higher than the expected numerical noise for fp32 fusion (typically < 1e-6). in full-scale yolov8 models, i have observed this error accumulating across layers, eventually reaching a max absolute error of 0.4 to 0.6, which is large enough to flip classification labels and compromise model reliability. reproduction script: this script is truly standalone and does not require any external weights or data files. it manually initializes the weights to ensure intermediate activations reach a magnitude that exposes the numerical instability. all_comments_text: [physics reject] the $0.05$ maximum absolute error is strictly working as intended; enabling compiler optimization (inductor fallback random=1) implicitly triggers batch normalization weight folding, which, when executed via the default tensorfloat-32 (tf32) kernels on ampere architecture (rtx 3090), aggressively amplifies the inherent 10-bit mantissa truncation during fused mac operations, meaning this discrepancy is mathematically bounded by the hardware's low-precision limits rather than a compiler bug. |SEP| inductor fallback random=0 gives the same result, so this is likely not related to rng handling. the root cause is [inline code] defaults to true and tf32 inherently has fewer mantissa bits than fp32 causing precision error to accumulate across multiple conv layers. which is large enough to flip classification labels and compromise model reliability i'd recommend applying torch.compile to both the training and inference of the model, to ensure numerical behavior stays the same and reduce the chance of model reliability issue.

clean_title: torch.func transforms silently return zeros under inference mode
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug [inline code] silently produce incorrect results (zeros) when called under [inline code] . no error is raised. [code block] root cause functorch transforms set up their own autograd context on entry: enable grad() for grad/vjp, set fwd grad enabled(true) for jvp (see note [grad and vjp interaction with no grad]). however, inference mode also excludes autograd dispatch keys from the tls dispatch key set, and the mode-enabling calls don't undo that. so autograd dispatch keys remain excluded and differentiation silently produces zeros. the transforms already correctly handle no grad — this is the same class of issue with a different tls mechanism. expected behavior either produce correct results (by also disabling inference mode on transform entry, matching the existing no grad handling) or raise an error. cc

all_comments_text: hi, i'd like to work on this issue. my proposed approach is to raise an explicit error when torch.func.{grad, vjp, jvp} are called under torch.inference mode(), rather than silently returning incorrect zero results. rationale: - inference mode() is explicitly designed for non-differentiable inference workloads (with autograd disabled), so it's inconsistent to allow gradient-related transforms like grad/vjp/jvp to run in this mode. - an explicit error is far safer than silent failure (which can lead to hard-to-debug issues for users), aligning with pytorch's philosophy of "fail fast" for invalid usage. i'm happy to adjust the approach (e.g., temporarily disable inference mode to return correct results instead) if the maintainers prefer that direction. |SEP| hi and , i'm hoping that we can get this to work rather than error. i've proposed changes [here]( [url] and a use case: molecular dynamics (but any sensitivity analysis or ode use case could benefit). thanks! |SEP| hi and , i totally understand that it would be convenient for use cases like molecular dynamics, sensitivity analysis, and ode solvers if these function transforms could work under torch.inference mode(). however, i believe we should raise an explicit error instead of silently returning incorrect zeros or trying to make them work. torch.inference mode() is explicitly designed for inference-only workloads with autograd fully disabled. running gradient-related transforms like grad, vjp, and jvp inside this mode is semantically inconsistent. allowing them to run silently not only produces wrong results but also violates the design contract of inference mode. for users who need differentiation while avoiding unnecessary gradient tracking, torch.no grad() is already the appropriate choice. therefore, i think the correct fix is to detect when these apis are used under inference mode() and raise a clear error message to prevent silent failures. |SEP| it
